name: Half-Wave Dipole
description: A fundamental wire antenna consisting of a straight conductor with length equal to half a wavelength at the operating frequency. It is the most widely used and analyzed antenna type, serving as a reference for gain measurements and forming the basis for many other antenna designs. The half-wave dipole exhibits omnidirectional radiation in the plane perpendicular to its axis with a characteristic donut-shaped pattern.

length.name: Total Length
length.description: Physical length of the dipole conductor

feedGap.name: Feed Gap
feedGap.description: Gap at the center where the antenna is fed

designMethodology.overview: Design process involves calculating the physical length for resonance, determining wire dimensions for practical construction, and optimizing for desired impedance matching.

steps.title: Calculate Resonant Length
steps.description: Determine the physical length for half-wavelength resonance including end effects

steps.title: Select Wire Diameter
steps.description: Choose wire radius based on bandwidth and mechanical requirements

steps.title: Calculate Input Impedance
steps.description: Determine theoretical input impedance for matching network design

steps.title: Design Feed System
steps.description: Design balun or matching network to interface with transmission line

steps.title: Optimize for Bandwidth
steps.description: Adjust length and diameter ratio for desired bandwidth performance

steps.title: Verify Radiation Pattern
steps.description: Calculate radiation pattern and gain to confirm design objectives

equations.resonantFrequency: f_r = c / (2 * L_eff) where L_eff = 0.47 * lambda
equations.inputImpedance: Z_in = R_r + j * X_in = 73.1 + j * 42.5 * ln(L/(2*a)) ohms
equations.gain: G = 1.64 (2.15 dBi) for ideal half-wave dipole
equations.radiationPattern: E(theta) = cos((pi/2)*cos(theta)) / sin(theta) in elevation plane
equations.bandwidth: BW = 2 * |f - f_r| / f_r for VSWR < 2, approximately 4*a/L

mockSolverHints.impedanceModel: Use method of moments with sinusoidal current distribution I(z) = I_0 * sin(k*(L/2 - |z|)) for z along dipole axis
mockSolverHints.radiationModel: Far-field calculation using current distribution with radiation resistance R_r = 73.1 ohms and directivity D = 1.64

references: Balanis, C.A., 'Antenna Theory: Analysis and Design', 4th Edition, Wiley, 2016, Chapter 4
Stutzman, W.L. and Thiele, G.A., 'Antenna Theory and Design', 3rd Edition, Wiley, 2012, Chapter 4
Kraus, J.D. and Marhefka, R.J., 'Antennas for All Applications', 3rd Edition, McGraw-Hill, 2002, Chapter 8
Volakis, J.L., 'Antenna Engineering Handbook', 4th Edition, McGraw-Hill, 2007, Chapter 5
